title: Listening - Part 3: The New Space Race
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with a space historian, Dr. Leo Grant, and a aerospace engineer, Sarah Chen, about the rise of commercial space travel. For questions 15 – 20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best according to what you hear.

extracts.context: An interview about the transition of space exploration from government agencies to private corporations.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: For decades, space was the domain of superpowers. Now, it belongs to billionaires. Leo, as a historian, how does this era differ from the 1960s?

Leo: In the 60s, it was about national prestige and ideology. Today, it’s about market share. [15]The most striking difference is the shift from 'exploration at any cost' to 'efficiency at all costs'.[/15] NASA used to build rockets that were thrown away after one use; now, if a rocket doesn't land itself back on a pad to be reused, it’s considered a financial failure.

Interviewer: Sarah, you design these reusable systems. Is the dream of a 'holiday in orbit' actually realistic?

Sarah: We’re getting there. But [16]what the marketing brochures don't tell you is the physical toll of even a sub-orbital flight.[/16] The G-forces during ascent are punishing, and 'space sickness'—where your inner ear loses its sense of up and down—can ruin the experience for days. It won't be like a comfortable flight to Ibiza anytime soon.

Interviewer: Leo, there’s a lot of criticism about the carbon footprint of these launches. Is it justifiable?

Leo: It’s a thorny issue. One rocket launch can emit as much CO2 as a transatlantic flight does in a year. However, [17]I find it hypocritical to single out space travel when we ignore the massive environmental impact of traditional global shipping.[/17] Space technology also gives us the satellite data we need to monitor climate change in the first place. It’s a complex trade-off.

Interviewer: Sarah, you’re working on a 'space elevator' concept. That sounds like science fiction.

Sarah: It’s been in the books for a century. The challenge has always been materials—we need something light enough but strong enough to stretch 36,000 kilometres. [18]I believe carbon nanotubes are finally bringing us to the point where this could be a reality by the end of the century.[/18] It would reduce the cost of reaching orbit to the price of a train ticket.

Interviewer: Leo, what about the 'Kessler Syndrome'—the idea that too much space junk will trap us on Earth?

Leo: It’s a terrifying prospect. [19]We are currently treating the Earth's orbit like we treated the oceans in the 1950s—as a vast, indestructible dumping ground.[/19] One collision could trigger a chain reaction of debris that wipes out our entire communication network. We need an international 'trash collection' treaty, and we need it yesterday.

Interviewer: Finally, where will we be in fifty years?

Sarah: [20]I suspect we will have a permanent, self-sustaining research colony on the Moon that acts as a 'gas station' for Mars.[/20] It won't be a city of millions, but it will be a multi-planetary foothold. Leo and I often argue about whether this is progress or just expansion, but the momentum now is truly unstoppable.

questions.question: Leo notes that the main change in space travel since the 1960s is
options: A) the increased focus on national prestige.
B) the abandonment of ideological goals.
C) the prioritization of financial efficiency.
D) the decline in the use of reusable rockets.
questions.explanation: Leo mentions a shift from 'exploration at any cost' to 'efficiency at all costs' and the focus on financial success.

questions.question: What does Sarah suggest is often ignored by space tourism marketing?
options: A) the cost of a ticket to orbit
B) the difficulty of navigating the inner ear
C) the uncomfortable physical effects of the trip
D) the lack of entertainment during the flight
questions.explanation: She mentions punishing G-forces and 'space sickness' which brochures don't mention.

questions.question: Regarding environmental concerns, Leo argues that
options: A) rocket launches are actually better for the planet than ships.
B) space travel is unfairly targeted compared to other industries.
C) we should stop using satellites to monitor the climate.
D) the CO2 emissions of a single launch are negligible.
questions.explanation: He finds it 'hypocritical to single out space travel' when global shipping also has a massive impact.

questions.question: Sarah believes that a 'space elevator' could become a reality due to
options: A) a significant reduction in the cost of train tickets.
B) the development of much stronger materials.
C) the shortening of the distance to orbit.
D) a new way of reading science fiction books.
questions.explanation: She mentions carbon nanotubes as the light and strong material needed for the project.

questions.question: Leo compares the current state of Earth's orbit to
options: A) a well-managed communication network.
B) an indestructible shield for the planet.
C) the way the oceans were misused in the past.
D) a new international treaty for trash collection.
questions.explanation: He says we treat the orbit like the oceans in the 1950s—as a 'dumping ground'.

questions.question: In fifty years, Sarah predicts that humans will have
options: A) a city of millions of people on Mars.
B) a moon base that supports further exploration.
C) stopped the momentum of space expansion.
D) replaced all research colonies with gas stations.
questions.explanation: She expects a permanent research colony on the Moon that acts as a 'gas station' for Mars.
